summary: Ancient Greek philosopher, author of the Republic and numerous dialogues featuring Socrates. Associated with the Theory of Forms, the Allegory of the Cave, the ideal city Kallipolis, the tripartite soul, and the founding of the Academy. His work gave rise to Neoplatonism (Plotinus, Porphyry) and the concept of mathematical Platonism.

works.name: The Republic and the Ideal City (Kallipolis)
works.description: The Republic is Plato's most frequently clued work, centering on justice and the ideal city. Kallipolis has three classes (workers, auxiliaries, guardians) based on a 'noble lie' dividing citizens into gold, silver, and iron/brass castes. Philosopher-kings rule; poets are expelled. The guardian class shares wives and receives training in gymnastics and music. The Allegory of the Cave imagines prisoners watching shadows on a wall, with one escaping to see the sun (representing the Form of the Good). The Republic ends with the Myth of Er, featuring the Spindle of Necessity and the choice of lives before reincarnation.

clues.clue: Allegory of the Cave: prisoners watching shadows on a wall; one escapes to see the sun
examples: imagines people watching shadows projected onto the wall of a cave
an analogy describes chained prisoners who think the shadows they see on a wall are the whole world

clues.clue: Ideal city has three classes: workers, auxiliaries, guardians ruled by philosopher-kings

clues.clue: Noble Lie / Allegory of Metals: gold, silver, iron/brass castes
examples: relies upon a false myth of origin that states that different races of men originate from iron, brass, gold, and silver

examples: Poets are expelled from this place
the banishment of the poets

examples: The Republic ends with the Myth of Er

clues.clue: Guardians share wives; women participate equally in education and battle
examples: A wife-sharing group of people in this place receives extensive training in gymnastics and music

examples: Glaucon had earlier propounded the social contract theory of justice before discussing the Ring of Gyges

clues.clue: Hannah Arendt criticized the Republic for extending ontology to politics but praised the Laws (5,040 citizens)

clues.clue: Metaphysical critique of poetry: God creates Form of furniture, carpenter creates furniture, painter copies it (three removes from truth)
examples: the example of God creating the Form of furniture, a carpenter creating furniture, and finally, a painter making a representation of a bed

clues.clue: Foundation of virtue ethics; argues philosopher kings must have wisdom, courage, justice, temperance
examples: argues philosopher kings must have the virtues of wisdom, courage, justice and temperance

works.name: The Tripartite Soul
works.description: Plato's theory that the soul has three parts is clued through the Republic and the Phaedrus. In the Republic, Leontius's simultaneous desire and disgust upon seeing corpses demonstrates that the soul undergoes 'opposites in relation to the same thing.' Homer's line about Odysseus striking his chest and speaking to his heart concludes the argument. In the Phaedrus, the tripartite soul is illustrated by a charioteer controlling two horses of opposing natures.

clues.clue: Leontius's desire and disgust upon seeing corpses demonstrates the soul's three parts
examples: Leontius's simultaneous desire and disgust upon seeing corpses supports this theory

clues.clue: Homer's line about Odysseus striking his chest concludes the argument in the Republic
examples: Homer's line 'he struck his chest and spoke to his heart' while describing the temperance of Odysseus

clues.clue: Phaedrus: charioteer allegory with two horses of opposing natures
examples: the tripartite soul is illustrated with two horses of opposing natures controlled by the same charioteer

works.name: Neoplatonism
works.description: Neoplatonism, the revival and reinterpretation of Plato's ideas, is heavily clued. Plotinus wrote the Enneads (compiled by Porphyry), conceiving a spiritual cosmology of the One, the Intelligence (nous), and the Soul. Porphyry wrote the Isagoge and Against the Christians. Iamblichus advanced theurgy. Proclus wrote the Elements of Theology. The tradition spread through the Islamic world via Arabic translations and the pseudo-Aristotelian Theology of Aristotle. al-Kindi, al-Farabi, and Suhrawardi were influenced. Marsilio Ficino revived it at the Florentine Academy, coining 'Platonic love' and writing Three Books on Life. Cambridge Platonists (Cudworth, More, Conway) continued the tradition in the 17th century.

clues.clue: Plotinus: the Enneads, compiled by Porphyry; cosmology of the One, nous/Intelligence, and the Soul
examples: the founding text, the Enneads, was compiled by Porphyry
conceived of a spiritual cosmology involving The One, the Intelligence, and the Soul

clues.clue: Porphyry: wrote the Isagoge (logic textbook), compiled the Enneads, wrote Against the Christians
examples: This author of the Isagoge edited and published the Enneads of his teacher Plotinus

clues.clue: Iamblichus advanced theurgy (divine magic); responded to Porphyry in On the Egyptian Mysteries
examples: Iamblichus advanced the rituals of theurgy in order to achieve henosis

clues.clue: Theology of Aristotle: actually a paraphrase of Plotinus's Enneads, transmitted to Islamic world
examples: The so-called Theology of Aristotle paraphrases texts from this philosophical tradition

clues.clue: Marsilio Ficino: revived Neoplatonism at Florentine Academy; coined 'Platonic love'; Three Books on Life; prisca theologia
examples: Marsilio Ficino helped revive what tradition, which the Florentine Academy promoted

clues.clue: Justinian closed the Academy in Athens
examples: this school's chief institution in Athens was shut down by Justinian I

clues.clue: al-Kindi used emanations from the One; al-Farabi modeled Virtuous City on the Republic

clues.clue: Cambridge Platonists: Cudworth (Plastic Nature), More (Spirit of Nature), Conway (influenced Leibniz's monads)
examples: Ralph Cudworth's principle concerning how God interacts with material objects
Conway posited 'middle nature' to explain how God communicates with matter

clues.clue: Augustine introduced Neoplatonic idea that evil is merely the absence of good
examples: This thinker introduced to Christian philosophy the Neoplatonic idea that evil is merely the absence of good

clues.clue: Pseudo-Dionysius applied Neoplatonic metaphysics to Christianity (On the Celestial Hierarchy)
examples: This thinker applied Neoplatonic metaphysics to Christian philosophy in works like On the Celestial Hierarchy

works.name: The Academy and Academic Skepticism
works.description: Plato founded the Academy in a grove outside Athens. Under later scholarchs Arcesilaus and Carneades, it became the center of Academic Skepticism, promoting reliance on the 'probable' (pithanon) in the face of acatalepsia (incomprehensibility). Carneades opposed Stoic katalepsis and gave contradictory lectures on justice in Rome. The Academy ended when Philo of Larissa fled to Rome during the First Mithridatic War. Justinian later closed its Neoplatonic successor. A plucked chicken was famously brought to the Academy by Diogenes to ridicule Plato's definition of man as a 'featherless biped.'

clues.clue: Plato founded the Academy in a grove outside Athens
examples: name this school in a grove outside Athens that was founded by Plato

clues.clue: Arcesilaus and Carneades turned Academy toward skepticism; promoted pithanon (the probable) and acatalepsia
examples: scholarchs like Arcesilaus and Carneades, this place names the more extreme of the two main traditions of ancient skepticism

clues.clue: Carneades: plank thought experiment (self-defense killings); contradictory lectures on justice in Rome
examples: This rival of Chrysippus names a 'plank' in a thought experiment used to discuss self-defense killings

clues.clue: Philo of Larissa fled to Rome during First Mithridatic War, ending the Academy
examples: This institution ended during the First Mithridatic War when Philo of Larissa fled to Rome

clues.clue: Diogenes plucked a chicken and brought it to the Academy: 'Behold, Plato's man!' (featherless biped)
examples: a plucked chicken was once brought to ridicule a definition of man as a 'featherless biped'

works.name: Mathematical Platonism
works.description: Mathematical Platonism (or mathematical realism) is the position that mathematical objects like numbers have mind-independent existence, analogous to Plato's Theory of Forms. The Quine-Putnam indispensability argument is the main argument for it: we should have ontological commitments to entities necessary for our best scientific theories. Paul Benacerraf argued against it in 'What Numbers Could Not Be' using the educations of Johnny and Ernie. Stewart Shapiro proposed a 'working' version. Frege assumed this position in the Grundlagen. Formalism directly opposes it.

clues.clue: Quine-Putnam indispensability argument: math is necessary for best scientific theories, so we must accept mathematical objects exist
examples: the Quine-Putnam indispensability thesis is generally understood as an argument for this philosophical position

clues.clue: Benacerraf's 'What Numbers Could Not Be': Johnny and Ernie thought experiment against mathematical Platonism
examples: the children Ernie and Johnny are taught conflicting definitions of these things in a thought experiment

clues.clue: Stewart Shapiro proposed a 'working' form of this view
examples: Stewart Shapiro's 'working' form of this view says practitioners should behave as if this view is true

clues.clue: Frege argued for it in the Grundlagen
examples: In the Grundlagen, Frege assumes the truth of certain simple identity statements to argue for this view

clues.clue: Formalism (Hilbert's treatment of geometry) directly opposes it
examples: Formalism, which directly opposes this theory, claims its subject proceeds purely by syntactic transformations

clues.clue: Hartry Field's nominalization of Newtonian physics to counter the indispensability argument
examples: Hartry Field created a 'nominalization' of Newtonian physics based upon qualitative relationships between points in spacetime

works.name: Timaeus
works.description: The Timaeus is one of Plato's most influential dialogues, discussing cosmology and the creation of the universe. The Demiurge creates the world because 'nothing becomes or changes without cause.' The four elements correspond to geometric bodies (Platonic solids). The Demiurge creates the world-soul by combining Existence, Sameness, and Difference. The Timaeus was the only Platonic dialogue known in medieval times through Calcidius's Latin translation, where it influenced the equivocation of the Holy Spirit with the 'World-Soul.' The story of Atlantis appears in Timaeus and Critias.

clues.clue: Demiurge creates the universe; four elements correspond to geometric bodies (Platonic solids)
examples: claims that the four elements correspond to specific Platonic solids
envisions the universe as a receptacle filled with four elements

clues.clue: Only Platonic dialogue known in medieval times through Calcidius's translation
examples: translated by Calcidius that was the only one of its type known in medieval times

clues.clue: Demiurge creates world-soul from Existence, Sameness, and Difference
examples: the demiurge created this thing by combining Existence, Sameness, and Difference

clues.clue: Story of Atlantis appears in Timaeus and Critias
examples: The story Atlantis can be found in Critias and this Platonic dialogue

clues.clue: Equivocation of Holy Spirit with 'World-Soul' from medieval reading
examples: The equivocation of the Holy Spirit with the 'World-Soul' came from a reading of one of these texts

works.name: Plato's Unwritten Doctrines and Letters
works.description: Plato's unwritten or esoteric doctrines (agrapha dogmata) are debated by the Tubingen School (Hans Kramer, Konrad Gaiser), who argue these theories underlie all of Plato's thought. According to Aristotle's Metaphysics, they posit two ultimate principles: the One and the Indefinite Dyad. Giovanni Reale defends them; Harold Cherniss rejects them. Plato's letters include the Seventh Letter, describing his disastrous attempt to advise Dionysius II of Syracuse, and are numbered 309a-363e in the Stephanus pagination.

clues.clue: Tubingen School (Kramer, Gaiser) argues unwritten doctrines underlie all Plato's thought
examples: Hans Kramer and Konrad Gaiser controversially contend that these theories underlie all their creator's thought

clues.clue: Two ultimate principles: the One and the Indefinite Dyad (from Aristotle's Metaphysics)
examples: there are two ultimate principles, the One and the Indefinite Dyad, which generate the Forms

clues.clue: Seventh Letter: disastrous attempt to advise Dionysius II, tyrant of Syracuse
examples: a disastrous attempt to advise Dionysius II, the tyrant of Syracuse

clues.clue: Stephanus pagination used for Plato's works (and Plutarch)
examples: passages in this philosopher's writings are often referred to using the Stephanus pagination

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: General identification clues: author of the Republic and other dialogues featuring Socrates; founded the Academy; teacher of Aristotle. Derrida reinvigorated interest in Plato's use of khora (space/receptacle). Plato characterized knowledge as justified true belief. In the Meno, an uneducated slave solves a geometrical puzzle, demonstrating knowledge as recollection. The concept of 'Platonic love' was coined by Ficino. Diogenes Laertius claims Plato's common name means 'broad-shouldered,' a wrestling nickname. Plato defined man as a 'featherless biped,' which Diogenes the Cynic refuted with a plucked chicken.

clues.clue: Author of the Republic; teacher of Aristotle; student of Socrates
examples: name this Greek philosopher, the author of the Republic

clues.clue: Theory of Forms: abstract, perfect entities that physical things imperfectly copy
examples: Socrates identifies the highest level of reality with abstract forms

clues.clue: Characterized knowledge as justified true belief; also as recollection (Meno: slave solves geometry)
examples: generally credited as the first philosopher to characterize knowledge as justified true belief

clues.clue: Derrida: reinvigorated interest in Plato's use of khora (space/receptacle)
examples: Jacques Derrida reinvigorated interest in this philosopher's use of the term khora

clues.clue: Defined man as 'featherless biped'; Diogenes refuted with plucked chicken
examples: Plato defined man as a 'featherless biped' Diogenes the Cynic plucked what sort of animal to disprove him

clues.clue: Name means 'broad-shouldered,' possibly a wrestling nickname (Diogenes Laertius)
examples: Diogenes Laertius claims that this philosopher's common name, meaning 'broad-shouldered,' was a wrestling nickname

clues.clue: Plato's beard: Quine's name for the problem of nonbeing ('nonbeing must in some sense be')
examples: Quine's name for the 'old Platonic riddle' in which 'nonbeing must in some sense be'

comprehensive_summary:
Plato is one of the most heavily clued philosophers in quizbowl, with his influence spanning the Republic, the Academy, Neoplatonism, and mathematical Platonism. The Republic is the central text, featuring the ideal city Kallipolis with its three-class system of workers, auxiliaries, and guardians under philosopher-kings. The 'noble lie' assigns citizens to gold, silver, or iron/brass castes. Poets are famously expelled in Book X, and the Allegory of the Cave depicts prisoners watching shadows on a wall. The Republic ends with the Myth of Er, where souls choose their next lives at the Spindle of Necessity before reincarnation.

The tripartite soul, argued in the Republic and illustrated in the Phaedrus with the charioteer allegory, divides the soul into rational, spirited, and appetitive parts. Leontius's simultaneous desire and disgust at corpses demonstrates this division.

Plato founded the Academy, which later became the center of Academic Skepticism under Arcesilaus and Carneades. The Academy was closed by Justinian. Diogenes the Cynic's plucked chicken incident at the Academy, refuting Plato's definition of man as a 'featherless biped,' is one of the most frequently clued anecdotes.

Neoplatonism is an enormous cluster of clues. Plotinus's Enneads (compiled by Porphyry) establish a cosmology of the One, nous, and the Soul. The tradition spread through Islam via the pseudo-Aristotelian Theology of Aristotle, influencing al-Kindi, al-Farabi, and Suhrawardi. Marsilio Ficino revived it at the Florentine Academy, coining 'Platonic love' and using the concept of prisca theologia. Cambridge Platonists (Cudworth, More, Conway) continued the tradition. Iamblichus advanced theurgy; Proclus wrote the Elements of Theology.

Mathematical Platonism holds that numbers and mathematical objects exist independently. The Quine-Putnam indispensability argument supports it; Benacerraf's Johnny and Ernie thought experiment opposes it. Frege, Shapiro, and Godel have all engaged with this position.

The Timaeus discusses cosmology through the Demiurge, Platonic solids, and the world-soul. It was the only Platonic dialogue known in medieval times through Calcidius's translation. Plato's unwritten doctrines, debated by the Tubingen School, posit the One and the Indefinite Dyad. The Seventh Letter describes Plato's failed attempt to advise Dionysius II of Syracuse.
recursive_suggestions: Republic (Plato) - heavily clued as its own answerline
Neoplatonism - massive topic deserving its own page
Symposium - separate answerline with Diotima and love speeches
